42F — Cranial Fossae & Dural Folds 42G — Meninges, Supply & Innervation 42H — Brain Lobes, Ventricles & CSF 📷 Figure Gallery
LO 42F
Bones of the Three Cranial Fossae, Dural Folds, and Formation of Dural Sinuses

The Three Cranial Fossae

The floor of the cranial cavity is organized into three stepped fossae, each housing specific brain regions and marked by characteristic bony features. They are like stadium tiers — anterior is highest, posterior is lowest.

FossaKey BonesKey Foramina / StructuresContents
Anterior Orbital plate of frontal; cribriform plate of ethmoid; lesser wing of sphenoid (posterior border) Cribriform foramina (CN I); foramen cecum; anterior & posterior ethmoidal foramina Frontal lobes; olfactory bulbs & tracts
Middle Greater wing of sphenoid; body of sphenoid (sella turcica); squamous & petrous temporal Optic canal (CN II, ophthalmic a.); SOF (CN III, IV, V1, VI, sup. ophthalmic v.); foramen rotundum (V2); foramen ovale (V3); foramen spinosum (middle meningeal a.); foramen lacerum; carotid canal Temporal lobes; pituitary gland; cavernous sinus
Posterior Occipital bone; petrous & mastoid temporal; body of sphenoid (clivus) Foramen magnum (medulla, vertebral aa., CN XI spinal root); internal acoustic meatus (CN VII, VIII); jugular foramen (CN IX, X, XI cranial; sigmoid + inf. petrosal sinus → IJV); hypoglossal canal (CN XII) Brainstem; cerebellum; CN IV-XII emergence

Dural Folds (Infoldings)

The inner periosteal and meningeal layers of dura separate to create rigid shelves and partitions that support and compartmentalize the brain. Dr. Willard's dissections show these directly: the outer layer crosses as a flat sheet while the inner layers curve inward to form these folds.

⚔️
Falx Cerebri
Sickle-shaped; sagittal plane
Attaches: crista galli → internal occipital protuberance. Separates L and R cerebral hemispheres. Superior border = superior sagittal sinus; inferior border = inferior sagittal sinus.
🪁
Tentorium Cerebelli
Tent-shaped; horizontal plane
Attaches: petrous ridges + posterior clinoid (anterior) and transverse sulci of occipital (posterior). Separates cerebral hemispheres from cerebellum. Free medial edge = incisura (tentorial notch) through which the midbrain passes.
🔪
Incisura Tentorii
Tentorial notch — the sharp free border
Dr. Willard: "The incisor is the sharp border of the tentorium — it is up against the brain stem." Uncal herniation → uncus pressed against this edge → CN III compression.
🏕️
Falx Cerebelli
Small; below tentorium
Partially separates the two cerebellar hemispheres. Attaches to internal occipital crest. Occipital sinus runs in its posterior border.
💺
Diaphragma Sellae
Horizontal shelf; roof of sella
Covers the pituitary fossa; small central aperture transmits the pituitary stalk (infundibulum).
📌 Dr. Willard's Key Observation
The dura is a double layer: an outer endosteal layer (periosteum of cranium) and an inner meningeal layer. As they approach the midline at the sagittal suture, the inner layer peels away and curves inward — the two inner layers from each side fuse to form the falx cerebri. Where inner layers of dura separate, they create the dural venous sinuses.
COA Fig 8.30 Interior of the base of the cranium — all three cranial fossae labeled Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.29 Dural infoldings and dural venous sinuses — falx, tentorium, and sinus relationships Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Grant's Fig 7.40 Dissection of the floor of the cranial cavity — fossae, foramina, and key structures Grant's Dissector, 17e
Grant's Fig 7.41 Dural infoldings and dural venous sinuses — cadaveric dissection view Grant's Dissector, 17e
Grant's Fig 7.37 Reflection of temporalis and occipitofrontalis; cuts for calvaria removal Grant's Dissector, 17e

Formation of the Dural Venous Sinuses

Wherever the two leaves of dura separate, they create a rigid, endothelium-lined venous channel — a dural sinus. Because the walls are dural (not muscular), these cannot collapse — this is physiologically critical: CSF must drain from the subarachnoid space into the superior sagittal sinus via arachnoid granulations, which only works if the sinus pressure stays lower than CSF pressure. A collapsible vein would shut down this drainage.

SinusLocation / FoldDrains Into
Superior SagittalUpper border of falx cerebri; runs sagittally front → backConfluens sinuum (torcular Herophili)
Inferior SagittalFree inferior border of falx cerebriStraight sinus (with great cerebral vein)
StraightJunction of falx and tentoriumConfluens sinuum
Transverse (bilateral)Attached border of tentorium (in occipital groove)Sigmoid sinus
Sigmoid (bilateral)S-shaped; mastoid grooveInternal jugular vein at jugular foramen
Cavernous (bilateral)Body of sphenoid / sella turcicaSup. & inf. petrosal sinuses → IJV / basilar plexus
Superior PetrosalAttached border of tentorium along petrous ridgeTransverse/sigmoid sinus
Inferior PetrosalGroove between petrous bone & clivusIJV (directly, inside jugular foramen)
OccipitalFalx cerebelli → foramen magnumConfluens sinuum
SphenoparietalPosterior edge of lesser wing of sphenoidCavernous sinus
🩸 Epidural vs Subdural Bleeding — Relationship to Sinuses
Epidural: Arterial bleeding (middle meningeal a.) between dura and inner table of skull — has to forcibly strip the collagenous attachments of dura off bone. Classic: lucid interval after temporal blow.
Subdural: Venous bleeding. The cerebral bridging veins pass through the arachnoid membrane to empty into dural sinuses (especially superior sagittal). Rapid acceleration/deceleration tears them at the dural entry point — blood collects in the "potential space" between dura and arachnoid. Dr. Willard: "The arachnoid is attached to the vein but has split away from the dura."
COA Fig 8.31 Venous sinuses of dura mater — complete sinus system with tributaries Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.32 MIP MR venogram of dural venous sinuses — clinical imaging correlate Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Grant's Fig 7.36 Coronal section through superior sagittal sinus — meningeal layers and sinus wall Grant's Dissector, 17e
Grant's Fig 7.39 Circumferential cut to reflect dura mater — exposing underlying structures Grant's Dissector, 17e
LO 42G
Layers of Meninges, Blood Supply, Venous Drainage, and Innervation

The Three Meningeal Layers

From outermost to innermost: Dura mater → Arachnoid mater → Pia mater. The dura and arachnoid together are sometimes called the pachymeninges; the arachnoid and pia are the leptomeninges.

1. Dura Mater ("Tough Mother")

Thick, fibrous, two-layered (endosteal + meningeal). The outer endosteal layer is the periosteum of the inner cranial surface; the inner meningeal layer lines the brain proper. They are fused everywhere except where sinuses form. Dr. Willard: "Sometimes it's thought the dura is inert — but its vasculature is extensive and it is metabolically well alive."

  • Epidural space: Potential space between outer dura and bone (traversed by middle meningeal a.; no true space unless blood collects)
  • Subdural space: Potential space between inner dura and arachnoid; cells fused with gap junctions (EM study); easier to create with venous bleeding than to strip dura off bone

2. Arachnoid Mater ("Spider-like")

Two components: (a) membranous arachnoid — a thin avascular sheet pressed against the inner dura and fused to it by gap junctions in life; (b) arachnoid trabeculae — spiderweb-like filaments connecting to the pia. The subarachnoid space between membranous arachnoid and pia is filled with CSF. In death, CSF drains and arachnoid shrink-wraps onto gyri. Subarachnoid arteries (with aneurysm potential) and bridging veins course through this space.

3. Pia Mater ("Tender Mother")

Tightly adherent to every contour of brain surface. Cannot be separated from the brain — it is functionally part of the brain parenchyma. Follows vessels into sulci. Along with arachnoid trabeculae, it forms the inner boundary of the subarachnoid space.

COA Fig 8.28 Meninges and their relationship to calvaria, brain, and spinal cord Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.35 Leptomeninges — arachnoid, subarachnoid space, and pia relationships Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Grant's Fig 7.38 The cranial meninges opened — superior sagittal sinus; bridging veins Grant's Dissector, 17e
🩸 Hemorrhage Localisation Summary
TypeLayerSourceBloodClassic Cause
EpiduralBone → outer duraMiddle meningeal a.ArterialTemporal skull fracture, lucid interval
SubduralInner dura → arachnoidBridging veinsVenousAcceleration/deceleration; elderly (atrophy)
SubarachnoidArachnoid → pia (SAC)Berry aneurysm ruptureArterial"Worst headache of life"; neck stiffness
IntracerebralWithin brain parenchymaPerforating aa. (HTN)ArterialHypertensive crisis, basal ganglia common

Blood Supply of the Meninges

The dura is extensively vascularized (demonstrated by latex-painted dissections). Arterial supply by region:

  • Anterior fossa: Anterior meningeal branches of anterior ethmoidal a. (from ophthalmic) and meningeal branch of internal carotid
  • Middle fossa: Middle meningeal artery (largest; branch of maxillary a. via foramen spinosum) — runs in grooves on inner surface of temporal squama; its anterior branch lies close to pterion. Accessory meningeal a. (via foramen ovale)
  • Posterior fossa: Meningeal branches of vertebral a. (posterior meningeal); meningeal branches of ascending pharyngeal; meningeal branch of occipital a. (via jugular foramen & mastoid foramen)
  • Arachnoid & Pia: Avascular — nourished by CSF

Venous Drainage

Cerebral veins → dural sinuses → internal jugular vein (primarily). Key tributary veins:

  • Superior cerebral veins (8–12): drain superior, lateral, and medial cerebral surfaces → superior sagittal sinus. These are the bridging veins that tear in subdural hematoma.
  • Superficial middle cerebral vein: follows lateral fissure → cavernous sinus (or sphenoparietal)
  • Great cerebral vein of Galen: drains deep structures (basal ganglia, thalami, choroid plexuses) → joins inferior sagittal sinus to form straight sinus
  • Diploic veins: within skull cancellous bone — can communicate with emissary veins (scalp ↔ sinus)
  • Emissary veins: small connections through skull foramina; allow spread of scalp infection intracranially

Innervation of the Dura Mater

The brain parenchyma is pain-insensitive; the dura is the primary pain-sensitive meningeal layer. This is clinically fundamental — headaches from meningeal traction, sinusitis, and intracranial bleeds all originate from dural nociceptors.

RegionNerveParent
Anterior fossa floorAnterior & posterior ethmoidal nn.Ophthalmic (V1) via nasociliary
Middle fossa + falx (anterior)Nervus spinosus (meningeal br.)Mandibular (V3) → re-enters via foramen spinosum
Middle fossa (medial)Tentorial nerve (recurrent meningeal, Arnold's nerve)Ophthalmic (V1) — unique ascending branch before SOF
Posterior fossaMeningeal branches of CN X & C2/C3Vagus + upper cervical nerves
Posterior fossa (tentorium)Tentorial branchV1 (ophthalmic)
⚡ Clinical Pearl — Referred Pain
Because the posterior fossa dura is innervated by CN X and C2/C3, lesions there (posterior fossa tumors, cerebellar hemorrhage) can refer pain to the back of the head/neck (C2/C3 distribution) or to the ear (CN X auricular branch). Middle fossa dural irritation (e.g., temporal epidural) → temporal headache via V3.
COA Fig 8.33 Deep dissection of suboccipital region — meningeal blood supply and posterior fossa relations Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.34 Innervation of dura mater — regional nerve supply by cranial fossa Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
LO 42H
Lobes & Ventricles of the Brain; Blood Supply, Venous Drainage; Cavernous & Petrosal Sinuses; CSF Circulation

Lobes of the Cerebrum

Dr. Willard's dissections establish these landmarks. The sulcal boundaries are guides, not absolute walls — gyri are named after underlying myelinated bundles, and surface markings vary between individuals.

LobeBordersKey GyriKey Functions
Frontal Anterior to central sulcus; above lateral fissure Superior, middle, inferior frontal gyri (lateral); precentral gyrus; orbital gyri; gyrus rectus; medial frontal gyrus Motor cortex (precentral); executive function; Broca's area (pars triangularis + opercularis of dominant IFG)
Parietal Behind central sulcus; above lateral fissure; anterior to parieto-occipital sulcus Postcentral gyrus; supramarginal gyrus; angular gyrus Somatosensory cortex; spatial processing; Wernicke's area (dominant angular/supramarginal)
Temporal Below lateral fissure; anterior to occipital lobe Superior, middle, inferior temporal gyri; parahippocampal gyrus; uncus; fusiform gyri Auditory cortex (Heschl's); language comprehension; memory (hippocampus); face recognition
Occipital Behind parieto-occipital sulcus (arbitrary boundary laterally) Calcarine cortex (medial surface); lingual gyrus; cuneus Primary visual cortex (V1 around calcarine fissure)
Insula Deep to lateral fissure; not visible from surface Short and long insular gyri Visceral sensation; taste; autonomic; language
🔑 Limbic Lobe
Dr. Willard emphasized the limbic ring: parahippocampal gyrus → isthmus → cingulate gyrus — wrapping around the medial face of each hemisphere above the corpus callosum. Named by MacLean from earlier work by Papez (Columbus, Ohio). Associated with emotion, memory, and personality — lesions anywhere along this circuit alter emotional behavior.

Ventricular System

Overview

CSF-filled cavities derived from the embryonic neural tube lumen. Total CSF volume: ~150 mL; produced at ~0.35 mL/min (500 mL/day) — turned over ~3× daily. Production ≠ drainage → hydrocephalus.

Lateral Ventricles (L & R) → Foramina of Monro → 3rd Ventricle → Cerebral Aqueduct (Sylvius) → 4th Ventricle → Foramina of Luschka (×2 lateral) + Magendie (1 medial) → Subarachnoid space

Lateral Ventricles

C-shaped cavity following the curve of the cerebral hemisphere. Divided into: anterior horn (in frontal lobe), body (parietal), posterior horn (occipital), inferior horn (temporal). The body, posterior, and inferior horns meet at the trigone (atrium) — also called the confluence of the ventricle. The choroid plexus runs along the floor of the body and into the inferior horn.

Third Ventricle

Narrow midline slit between the two thalami (flanked by thalamus dorsally, hypothalamus ventrally). The hypothalamic sulcus divides them. The massa intermedia (thalamic adhesion) crosses the third ventricle in ~70% of people — it is a cell group, not a fiber bundle. The pineal gland projects from its posterior roof.

Cerebral Aqueduct

Narrow channel (~1.5 mm) through the midbrain connecting 3rd and 4th ventricles. Most common site for obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus — congenital stenosis, tumor compression (e.g., pinealoma), or post-inflammatory fibrosis.

Fourth Ventricle

Tent-shaped cavity between pons/medulla (floor) and cerebellum (roof). The floor contains the nuclei of CNs VI–XII. Three exits: paired lateral apertures of Luschka (into cerebellopontine angle cisterns) and median aperture of Magendie (into cisterna magna). Inferiorly, it continues as the central canal of the spinal cord (obliterated in adults).

💧 Choroid Plexus
Produces ~60% of CSF (remainder is extracellular fluid exuded from brain surface). Located on: floor of body + inferior horn of lateral ventricles; roof of 3rd ventricle; roof of 4th ventricle. Dr. Willard: "It's an ultrafiltrate of plasma extruded from this frond-like structure — like Bowman's capsule — hence 'brain pee'." Histologically resembles kidney glomerulus.
COA Fig 8.36 Structure of the brain — lobes, ventricles, and key surface landmarks Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e

CSF Circulation

After exiting the 4th ventricle, CSF fills the basal cisterns and circulates over the cerebral convexity and down around the spinal cord.

Drainage routes:

  • Arachnoid granulations (Pacchionian bodies) into the superior sagittal sinus and other sinuses — one-way pressure valves; CSF pressure must exceed sinus pressure for drainage (usually only a few mmHg differential)
  • Spinal arachnoid granulations associated with spinal nerve roots → vertebral plexus of veins (drains out each intervertebral foramen)
  • Minor: along cranial nerve sheaths (especially olfactory → nasal lymphatics)

Hydrocephalus Types

TypeMechanismVentricular PatternExample
Non-communicating (obstructive)Block within ventricular systemDilated proximal to block; normal distalAqueduct stenosis → dilated 3rd + laterals; foramen of Monro block → unilateral lateral dilation
CommunicatingImpaired CSF absorption (blocked arachnoid granulations)All ventricles uniformly dilatedPost-SAH (blood clogs granulations); post-meningitis; post-stroke (necrotic tissue debris)
Hydrocephalus ex vacuoBrain atrophy (not pressure-related)Ventricles enlarge into space left by lost tissueAlzheimer's; post-infarct
⚠️ Post-Stroke Hydrocephalus
Dr. Willard: Infarcted brain tissue necroses and dissolves, dumping debris into the CSF → can clog arachnoid granulations → post-stroke communicating hydrocephalus. "This is something you will have to watch for in a patient because it is common."

Blood Supply of the Brain

Arterial Supply — Overview

The brain receives blood from two pairs of arteries: the internal carotid arteries (anterior circulation) and the vertebral arteries (posterior circulation). These are linked by the Circle of Willis (circulus arteriosus).

Anterior Circulation (ICA)

  • Anterior cerebral artery (ACA): runs anteriorly along the genu of corpus callosum; supplies medial frontal and parietal lobes (leg & foot area of motor/sensory cortex). Linked by anterior communicating artery.
  • Middle cerebral artery (MCA): enters lateral fissure; supplies the entire lateral surface of the hemisphere (face, arm, language areas). Lenticulostriate arteries branch at origin → supply internal capsule, basal ganglia (most common stroke territory).
  • Posterior communicating artery (PComm): joins ICA to posterior cerebral — CN III runs between PCA and SCA; PComm aneurysms can compress CN III.
  • Anterior choroidal artery: small but critical; supplies posterior limb of internal capsule, optic tract, hippocampus.

Posterior Circulation (Vertebrobasilar)

  • Vertebral arteries: Enter skull via foramen magnum; give off posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) and anterior spinal artery before joining.
  • Basilar artery: Formed by union of vertebrals at pontomedullary junction; ascends along clivus. Branches: AICA (anterior inferior cerebellar), pontine perforators, SCA (superior cerebellar), then terminates as PCAs.
  • Posterior cerebral artery (PCA): Wraps around midbrain, supplies occipital lobe (visual cortex), medial temporal, thalamus. CN III passes between PCA and SCA.

The Cavernous Sinus — Detail

A trabeculated venous channel on each side of the body of the sphenoid, lateral to the pituitary fossa and sella turcica. Clinically important because of its contents and connections.

📦 Contents of the Cavernous Sinus
Within the sinus lumen: Internal carotid artery (siphon) + CN VI (abducens) — both bathed in venous blood.
In the lateral wall (superior to inferior): CN III (oculomotor), CN IV (trochlear), CN V1 (ophthalmic), CN V2 (maxillary).

Mnemonic: O TOM CAT — Oculomotor, Trochlear, Ophthalmic, Maxillary, Carotid Artery, Trochlear... or III IV V1 V2 | VI + ICA

Tributaries Into Cavernous Sinus

  • Superior and inferior ophthalmic veins (from orbit)
  • Superficial middle cerebral veins (via sphenoparietal sinus)
  • Central retinal vein (indirectly)
  • Pterygoid plexus (via emissary veins through foramen ovale) — route for spread of facial infections

Cavernous Sinus Drainage

Cavernous Sinus → Superior Petrosal Sinus (along petrous ridge → joins sigmoid → IJV) → Sigmoid Sinus → IJV
Cavernous Sinus → Inferior Petrosal Sinus (in groove between petrous & clivus) → IJV (directly, at jugular foramen)
L Cavernous ↔ Basilar Plexus (clivus) ↔ R Cavernous (interconnected — infection can cross midline)

Petrosal Sinuses

  • Superior petrosal sinus: Runs along the attached border of the tentorium on the petrous ridge; connects cavernous sinus to the junction of transverse and sigmoid sinuses. The tentorium attaches here — its attachment line can be seen in Dr. Willard's dissections as a blue line along the petrous bone.
  • Inferior petrosal sinus: Runs in the groove between the petrous temporal bone and the clivus (occipital); drains cavernous sinus directly into the IJV inside the jugular foramen. CN IX, X, XI also exit here.

Venous Drainage of the Brain — Summary

Venous drainage follows a predictable hierarchy from cerebral veins → dural sinuses → internal jugular vein.

SourceVein / SinusFinal Drainage
Lateral & superior cerebral surfaceSuperior cerebral veins (bridging veins)Superior sagittal sinus → confluens → transverse → sigmoid → IJV
Lateral fissure regionSuperficial middle cerebral v.Cavernous sinus (or sphenoparietal)
Deep brain (thalami, BG, choroid)Internal cerebral veins → great cerebral v. of GalenStraight sinus → confluens → transverse → sigmoid → IJV
OrbitSuperior + inferior ophthalmic veinsCavernous sinus → petrosal sinuses → IJV
Cavernous sinusSuperior & inferior petrosal sinusesIJV; basilar plexus (→ vertebral plexus)
